reasoning: Output B edges out Output A on completeness and usability. Both are high-quality contracts scoring 11/11 on their quality gates, and both satisfy the core requirements. The deciding factors are: (1) Output B includes a Deliverable Inventory section that explicitly enumerates all 11 deliverables before writing postconditions, making the contract's coverage auditable at a glance. (2) Output B provides 8 concrete expect() skeletons — full executable test bodies — not just test names, making the TDD handoff immediately actionable without interpretation. (3) Output B's traceability matrix uses full absolute file paths (e.g. apps/api/src/__tests__/shopifyWebhook.test.js) rather than bare filenames, eliminating any ambiguity about where tests live in the monorepo. (4) Output B's blast radius scan found a real security defect in a sibling file (shopifyRefundWebhookHandler.js using === for HMAC comparison instead of timingSafeEqual) and contracted it as PC-S8 — demonstrating active threat modeling, not just box-checking. (5) Output B's invariants table includes a Justification column explaining why each invariant applies to this specific feature, whereas Output A's invariants are bare assertions. Output A is excellent — it has a marginally richer service layer decomposition (separate normalization/persistence/query service PCs) and a more detailed Side Effects and Error Strategy section — but Output B's structural advantages in deliverable inventory, executable test skeletons, full file paths, and proactive defect discovery make it the superior contract for a build engineer picking this up cold.

strengths: 26 PCs with fine-grained service layer decomposition — normalization, persistence, and query services each get dedicated PCs (PC-S1 through PC-S9)
All 26 PCs have specific test file names and test names — fully traceable
Blast radius lists 7 same-file functions and 2 validation functions with line numbers
10 error cases covering HMAC (2), malformed payload (1), DB failure (2), pagination validation (2), auth (1), idempotency (1), cross-tenant (1)
Side effects section explicitly calls out log entries and transactional guarantees
Error strategy section includes retry policy and explicit transaction boundary diagram
7 NOT in Scope exclusions with a clear drift warning
Consumer map notes the architectural separation of concerns between OrderStatsWidget and the list endpoint
weaknesses: No Deliverable Inventory section — coverage of the 11 plan deliverables is implicit, not explicitly enumerated
No executable expect() skeletons — test names are present but no actual test code bodies; build engineer must write assertions from scratch
Traceability matrix uses bare filenames (e.g. shopifyWebhook.test.js) not full monorepo paths — ambiguous in a monorepo with apps/api/ and apps/admin/ structure
Invariants table has no Justification column — states what applies but not why it applies to this feature
No Postcondition Registry JSON path — no state handoff artifact for enterprise-build phase

strengths: Deliverable Inventory section explicitly maps all 11 plan deliverables to PC coverage before any postconditions are written
8 full executable expect() skeletons with real assertion logic (toBe, toHaveLength, toEqual, toBeInTheDocument) — immediately runnable
Traceability matrix uses full absolute file paths (apps/api/src/__tests__/shopifyWebhook.test.js) — zero ambiguity in monorepo
Blast radius scan actively found a security defect in shopifyRefundWebhookHandler.js (timing-unsafe HMAC comparison) and contracted it as PC-S8
Invariants table includes Justification column explaining why each invariant applies specifically to this feature
Consumer map includes grep command to confirm zero existing consumers (net-new feature verification)
Side effects section distinguishes intentional from unintentional side effects
NOT in Scope includes 6 explicit exclusions covering fulfillment webhooks, UI editing, rate limiting, OAuth, existing order routes, and notifications
Postcondition Registry JSON path specified for enterprise-build state handoff
Error cases include ERR-10 (oversized payload / 413) — defensive against DoS vectors not mentioned in the original plan
weaknesses: Service layer PCs (PC-S1 through PC-S7) conflate DB schema concerns (unique constraint, FK cascade) with service logic — some of these are migration PCs, not service PCs, which slightly blurs the layer separation
Consumer map uses full file paths but not line numbers — Output A's consumer map has file:line refs (e.g. hooks/useOrders.js:L14) which are more precise

details.note: Contract references docs/plans/ path but the contract file itself is not placed at a docs/contracts/ path — no file path header specifying target location
